summary:
The first handheld mobile telephone call was made by Motorola engineer Martin Cooper in New York City in April 1973, inaugurating the era of personal cellular communication. Commercial cellular networks launched in the early 1980s — in Japan in 1979, Scandinavia in 1981, and the United States in 1983 — initially serving business users who could afford the expensive handsets and call charges. Through the late 1980s and 1990s, successive generations of network technology and manufacturing efficiencies rapidly reduced costs.

The 1990s witnessed explosive growth in mobile phone adoption across the world, particularly with the introduction of the digital GSM standard. By 2000, there were approximately 740 million mobile phone subscribers globally, with penetration especially high in Scandinavia, East Asia, and Western Europe. In many developing countries, mobile networks leapfrogged fixed-line infrastructure entirely. The mobile phone also became a conduit for SMS text messaging, a communication mode that would reshape social interaction, and laid the technological and commercial foundation for the smartphone revolution of the following decade.
